I modified the code for my purpose. However, the image and masks not aligned properly.

```python
from imgaug import augmenters as iaa
from keras.preprocessing.image import array_to_img, img_to_array, load_img
from scipy import misc
import imageio 
import numpy as np

seq_img = iaa.Sequential([
    iaa.Affine(rotate=(-45, 45), order=1, mode="constant", cval=0, name="MyAffine")
])


seq_img = seq_img.localize_random_state()

seq_masks = iaa.Sequential([
    iaa.Affine(rotate=(-45, 45), order=0, mode="constant", cval=0, name="MyAffine")
])


img = load_img('temp/img/1_1.bmp')  # this is a PIL image
x = img_to_array(img)  # this is a Numpy array with shape (3, 150, 150)
images = x.reshape((1,) + x.shape)  # this is a Numpy array with shape (1, 3, 150, 150)

img = load_img('temp/labels/1_1.bmp')  # this is a PIL image
y = img_to_array(img)  # this is a Numpy array with shape (3, 150, 150)
masks = y.reshape((1,) + y.shape)  # this is a Numpy array with shape (1, 3, 150, 150)


i = 1
seq_masks_i = seq_masks.copy_random_state(seq_img, matching="name")
seq_masks_i = seq_masks_i.to_deterministic()
seq_img_i = seq_img.to_deterministic()
  
imgs_aug = seq_img_i.augment_images(images)
masks_aug = seq_masks_i.augment_images(masks)
    
imageio.imwrite("img_%06d.bmp"% (i,), np.squeeze(imgs_aug))
imageio.imwrite("msk_%06d.bmp"% (i,), np.squeeze(masks_aug))
 
```

Found this, while trying to replicate the issue.

```
★  Answer from https://stackoverflow.com/questions/20923663/unicodeencodeerror-ascii-codec-cant-encode-character-in-position-0-ordinal ★
When Python prints and output, it automatically encodes it to the target medium. If it is a file, UTF-8 
will be used as default and everyone will be happy, but if it is a terminal, Python will figure out the 
encoding the terminal is using and will try to encode the output using that one.
This means that if your terminal is using ascii as encoding, Python is trying to encode scissor char to 
ascii. Of course, ascii doesn't support it so you get Unicode decode error.
This is why you always have to explicitly encode your output . Explicit is better than implicit 
remember? To fix your code you may do:
import sys
sys.stdout.buffer.write(chr(9986).encode('utf8'))
This seems a bit hackerish. You can also set PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 before executing the 
script. I'am uncomfortable with both solutions. Probably your console doesn't support utf-8 and you 
see gibberish. But your program will be behaving correctly.
What I strongly recommend if you definitely need to show correct output on your console is to set 
your console to use another encoding, one that support scissor character. (utf-8 perhaps). On 
Linux, that can be achieve by doing: export lang=UTF_8 . On Windows you change the console's 
code page with chcp . Just figure out how to set utf8 in yours and IMHO that'll be the best solution.
print
sys.stdout.write
sys.stdout.buffer.write(("|\t "+ chr(9986) +" PySnipt'd " + chr(9986)+" \t|").encode('utf8'))
I suggest you to take a read at the docs to see what's going on under the hood with print function 
and with sys.stdout : http://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.stdin
Hope this helps!
```

This SO answer could probably explain the issue, but for me to verify if this is correct, I'll still need to set my own terminal's encoding to use ASCII (right now, it appears to default to UTF-8).

For reading array-valued data from MED, not a problem because in MED we have a name for each component via the `NOM` attribute of the field group: we have `SIXX, SIYY, SIZZ, SIXY, SIXZ, SIYZ`. If we prescribe that the tensor-valued data contained in `*_data` of `meshio` should always respect the VTK ordering convention, we just reverse the 5th and 6th component. This permits the natural conversion from MED to VTU while respecting the ordering.
```
points, cells, point_data, *_ = meshio.read("mesh.med")
meshio.write("mesh.vtu", points, cells, point_data)
```

For writing array-valued data to a MED file, since in `meshio` we don't have a *tag* for each component  of a tensor-valued data, we don't know for sure if we need to reverse the 5th and the 6th column. Suppose that a user stores for each node `(x, y)` the following nodal array-valued data `v`
```
v = (x, y, x+y, x-y, x**2, y**2)
```
this data doesn't have a tensor structure but unluckily possess 6 components. So for writing to MED maybe we should let the user do whatever he likes.

I stopped counting the percentage and now counting the standard error:
```python
standard_error = 1.04 / math.sqrt(2**12)  # 0.01625
```
By the link above [standard error](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_error), in particular the picture 
![standard_deviation_diagram](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13743726/42521648-0a45ad54-8472-11e8-8ff6-46abf4d6d0cc.png)
shows that the distribution of the standard error will be as follows:
```
68.2% - 1*standard_error # standard_error <= 0.01625
27.2% - 2*standard_error # 0.01625 < standard_error <= 0.0325
 4.2% - 3*standard_error # 0.0325 < standard_error <= 0.04875
```
In my example, the probabilities are:
```
('1*standard_error', 0.5)
('2*standard_error', 0.5)
('3*standard_error', 0.0)
```
They are not accurate due to the small number of samples (10 out of a 1M), but now it is clear why the error was more than 0.01625. I can manage inaccuracies with such probabilities:
```
68.2% - 1*standard_error # standard_error <= 0.01625
95.4% - 2*standard_error # standard_error <= 0.0325
99.6% - 3*standard_error # standard_error <= 0.04875
```

The `suppress` branch that I'm working on behaves similarly to what you said: a trigger key that must be released. In fact that's how most hotkey systems work: to trigger <kbd>ctrl+a</kbd> you have to press <kbd>ctrl</kbd> **then** <kbd>a</kbd>, and that's the trigger key. The concept applies to trigger-on-release hotkeys too.

Unfortunately things get hazier when there are multiple non-modifier keys together, like <kbd>tab+backspace</kbd> or <kbd>a+b</kbd>. I'm not sure how often people use hotkeys like this.

not supporting TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV is not necessarily a serious issue, depending on how well the client and server implement TLS 1.0 (by not supporting SSL 3.0 you already avoid the most glaring problems). However, good implementations cannot be guaranteed, and not supporting TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV is formally a weakness, even if it is not necessarily a vulnerability. That the weakness cannot be turned into a full exploit by attackers does not mean it does not exist.

In any case, you won't implement TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV because you want security; you will implement TLS_FALLBACK_SCSV because you want an A+. If you do not, then you will spend inordinate amounts of time explaining to many people that the "A+" grade is meaningless in that respect and that you can afford not to take it. In the long term, not howling with the wolves is too expensive.

The detailed description for the above said can be read from "https://security.stackexchange.com/questions/112531/is-tls-fallback-scsv-useless-if-only-tls-1-0-1-1-1-2-is-supported" which was answered by Tom Leek

Thank you a lot for pointing this out, but I would argue that this is not a bug in liac-arff, but in the [arff specification](http://weka.wikispaces.com/ARFF%20%28stable%20version%29). This behavior is not defined there, and I am very hesitant to add functionality here which is undocumented behavior of the WEKA arff reader. In fact, we deactivated the datasets in question on OpenML because they are not valid arff.

Also, this is a known bug in WEKA with a workaround on the WEKA side:
> Warning: There is a known problem saving SparseInstance objects from datasets that have string attributes. In Weka, string and nominal data values are stored as numbers; these numbers act as indexes into an array of possible attribute values (this is very efficient). However, the first string value is assigned index 0: this means that, internally, this value is stored as a 0. When a SparseInstance is written, string instances with internal value 0 are not output, so their string value is lost (and when the arff file is read again, the default value 0 is the index of a different string value, so the attribute value appears to change). To get around this problem, add a dummy string value at index 0 that is never used whenever you declare string attributes that are likely to be used in SparseInstance objects and saved as Sparse ARFF files.

One way to test it, *if you really need to* would be to mock the `distutils.spawn.find_executable('EnergyPlus')` call. Pass in the function to find EnergyPlus as a parameter, with `distutils.spawn.find_executable` as the default. Then, in the unit test, you can provide a known location (to a dummy file) with test data.

This does not really solve testing symlinks etc. Personally, I wouldn't write a test for this.

Hi wikiped, unfortunately we're not going to merge this feature, although anyone interested in it is welcome to pick it up from here.

In general, we advise being in touch before starting work on a new feature, to make sure the proposed feature is consistent with the existing API and so forth. This saves situations like this where we have to decline to merge and can yield a somewhat different approach that produces a mergeable feature.

In this case, we might be interested in a feature that allows getting a layout by name, but not by using that name as an attribute (dotted notation). The layout names are completely free-form, so there's no inherent consistency between layout names from one presentation to the next.

I think that might be getting a bit too involved, in that it might lead to surprising behaviour because the state is no longer in the command-line options, which is probably not a good idea. Also, given that your terminal can easily autocomplete to previous commands, and that you can set the default in the preferences, I think I'm leaning towards disliking this.

However, there's no reason why there couldn't be a device alias in the preferences file, so you could do -d 1, for example.

In response to your request, I have performed some performance profiling using the code in the new `numpy` branch. For reference, I have performed these tests on a system with the following specifications:
```
Intel Core i7 6700HQ
8GiB DDR4 memory
256GB NVMe SSD
```

Initiallly, I had chosen to use `numpy` because the compiled `numpy` library is generally much, much faster at performing even simple numeric operations in Python, and because the `pillow` library is compatible with it. After these results, however, I am reconsidering:
```
shift_hue:       24ms  27ms  28ms
shift_hue_numpy: 19ms  14ms 14ms
```
This means that using plain Python is significantly slower than using `numpy`, but the numeric difference is generally negligible. Even when importing the module at the top of the file instead of using a lazy import does not seem to have much of an impact:
```
examply.py, theme arc, no advanced theme
with_numpy:    276ms  288ms  273ms
without_numpy: 285ms  294ms  286ms
```
To me, that all seems to be within the margin of error. The import does not significantly slow down the starting of the `example.py` script.

My conclusion is that the usage of the `numpy` library does not significantly impact the performance of the library, and given how it is only used in a single function, it is best to remove the dependency entirely.

Hi,

In November `backtrader` received `filters` as a new functionality. Due to ticket #23 which also had missing bars with intraday data and this prevented synchronizing the same minute across days to compare the volume on a minute basis over days.

There is a post documenting a `SessionFilter` and a `SessionFiller` here: http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-11-21-data-filters/data-filling-filtering/

But it's not a panacea. Filling in missing data is a decision about *what to fill in*. And the answer depends on your specific scenario. This is something I haven't faced myself: my trading markets are so liquid that even during the quietest hours some trading takes place.

> In looking at this issue I found an issue with `SessionFiller` (the bar under evaluation could be moved too early into the stack) and have issued version 1.2.2.88 to correct it.

The sample from ticket #61 extended with a `SessionFiller`:

```python
from __future__ import (absolute_import, division, print_function,)
#                        unicode_literals)

import datetime

import backtrader as bt
import backtrader.feeds as btfeeds
import backtrader.filters as btfilters


class St(bt.Strategy):
    def next(self):
        dt00 = self.data0.datetime.datetime().isoformat()
        dt10 = self.data1.datetime.datetime().isoformat()
        print('%d: Intraday: %s - Day %s' % (len(self), dt00, dt10))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    cerebro = bt.Cerebro()

    cerebro.addstrategy(St)

    data0 = btfeeds.GenericCSVData(
        dataname='dump-5min.csv',
        timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Minutes,
        compression=5,
        sessionstart=datetime.datetime(1, 1, 1, 0, 0),  # only time is used
        sessionend=datetime.datetime(1, 1, 1, 23, 59),  # only time is used
    )
    data0.addfilter(btfilters.SessionFiller, fill_vol=0.0, fill_oi=0.0)

    data1 = btfeeds.GenericCSVData(dataname='dump-1day.csv',
                                   timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Days)   

    cerebro.adddata(data0)
    cerebro.adddata(data1)

    cerebro.run()
```

    def execute(self, req, arg=None, res=None):

        """
        self.reqs.put((req, arg or tuple(), res))

@warner yes, that will get you an ubuntu package published in the store.

What we have learned after many years of packaging in ubuntu is that our 6 months/2 years release cycle and heavy-weight packaging requirements to make debs consistent, don't fit all the projects. So we are trying to remove the distro as a middle man, and let upstreams publish directly whenever they want. The distro maintainers are a safeguard in terms of security and api compatibilities in the archive, so, to make them unnecessary, snaps are totally isolated by default, and bundle all their dependencies. 

We are working hard to make the process as light-weight as possible so it won't be a burden to the developers. You can even hook the snap build and push to something like travis or launchpad to have it published automatically to the edge channel every time your repository changes. It will be a small effort one time, and then you can forget about it. Then when you want to make a release, you just run one command to move a revision from the edge channel to another one, on your own timeline.

So, magic-wormhole has made it to the zesty deb archive of ubuntu, and it will be in the next release we make in april. But most of our users are in xenial, the latest long-term support release, and they won't be able to get easily the magic-wormhole deb. With snaps, you push one version and it will work in xenial and any later updates. And we are working on porting it back and getting it into other distros, so it will work in ubuntu 14.04, debian, fedora, everywhere.

I'm sorry, I've just noticed I wrote a lot :) But I'm excited about this, and it would be great for us if you can give us some feedback, taking into account your experience with maintainers. We are in heavy active development, so that will help us improving the developer journey in Linux.

If `LAYOUT` is not provided [`ws` setting](http://www.igordejanovic.net/parglare/parser/#ws) is used.
If `LAYOUT` is provided it **must** match between each token, including beginning and end of the input. I've added [a note in the docs](http://www.igordejanovic.net/parglare/grammar_language/#handling-whitespaces-and-comments-in-your-language). 

Controlling word boundaries is in parglare handled with a [special rule `KEYWORD`](http://www.igordejanovic.net/parglare/grammar_language/#handling-keywords-in-your-language) as the word boundary is not determined only by whitespaces. I think this mechanism should work in your case also.

Maybe this could be unified with #42 so that the same syntax would be used.

```
MyProduction: some terms and non-terms {>term1, >term2, shift};
```
`>term1` would mean that this production should be reduced if lookahead is `term1`. `shift` as default could be implied if `>` is used. In the case of non-terminals definition this would enhance static disambiguation during LR table calculation, while for terminal definition it would be used dynamically.

This could be used to resolve reduce-reduce conflicts like:
```
MyProduction: .... {>SomeOtherNonTerminalRule};
```
This could mean that in case of R/R conflict with `SomeOtherProduction`, `MyProduction` reduction would take place. This should be defined per production and with lookahead so the full syntax would be:

```
MyProduction: .... {>SomeOtherNonTerminalRule[3]:term1};
```
Where `SomeOtherNonTerminalRule[3]:term1` is the third production defined in the rule `SomeOtherNonTerminalRule` when lookahead is `term1`. Production index as well as lookahead may be optional. If not specified it would mean `any`.

I reverted all my changes to `util.py` and poked around some more for the issue I am seeing.

I confirmed that the problem relates to `%(xml_response)s`. Removing the line marked `###` causes the error message to go away.

```
    log_msg = str('''\
Retry: %(retry)s
Waited: %(wait)s
Timeout: %(timeout)s
Session: %(session_id)s
Thread: %(thread_id)s
Auth type: %(auth)s
URL: %(url)s
HTTP adapter: %(adapter)s
Allow redirects: %(allow_redirects)s
Response time: %(response_time)s
Status code: %(status_code)s
Request headers: %(request_headers)s
Response headers: %(response_headers)s
Request data: %(xml_request)s
Response data: %(xml_response)s   ####
''')
```

Using the information in the error message, I dumped the content of `r.content`:

```
  File "/home/earlchew/Local/pyenv/versions/2.7.9/lib/python2.7/logging/__init__.py", line 335, in getMessage
    msg = msg % self.args
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe2 in position 5115: ordinal not in range(128)
```

That position in `r.content` contains the following octets:

```
0000000   t   ;   L   e   t 342 200 231   s     
         74  3b  4c  65  74  e2  80  99  73  20 
```

That particular sequence `e2 80 99` corresponds to an apostrophe: `Let’s`

My understanding of the problem is this:
* The encoding of `r.content` is determined by the choice of `charset` in the headers. In this case it is `utf-8`.
* Although `log_msg` is created as `str` using 241b0fee, the `msg % self.args` operation seems to want to convert the `r.content` to unicode.
* Without guidance, the conversion uses the ascii code which fails on the utf-8 encoded characters in the text.

Digging into `logging.py` I added:

```
        if self.args:
            print '@@@', type(msg)
            msg = msg % self.args
```

Perplexingly, this shows:

```
@@@ <type 'str'>
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
    msg = msg % self.args
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe2 in position 5115: ordinal not in range(128)
```

I tried boiling this down to a test case:

```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

t = 'Let’s'
f = '%s'
x = f % t
```

But this works as advertised, so there's something else going on.

I can confirm that I do not see the error message when I use the changes I proposed.

I think generally if you end up replacing the Population class when implementing an evolution scheme, odds are that you're doing something different enough from NEAT that it might be an easier fit in another framework.  Of course, it could also be the case that neat-python is still too clumsy. :) 

In any case, it might be interesting to see what you have to do in order to get MAP-elites working; maybe there are some changes we can make to neat-python to make it easier.  Ideally all you'd need to do is provide a custom reproduction type and maybe some machinery around the fitness evaluation, but it's always more complicated than I think when I go to do something like that.  So far the only similar thing I've tried is the novelty-based fitness: https://github.com/CodeReclaimers/neat-python/blob/master/examples/picture2d/evolve_novelty.py

Thanks for the mention of DEAP, I hadn't seen that project before.  Maybe it will turn out that NEAT is easier to implement in DEAP than in pure Python. ;)

Thanks for mentioning this!  You are correct--there is nothing preventing disconnected networks from being created.  I haven't added any checks for this kind of network because I didn't want to create default behavior that could potentially interfere with evolution of a solution. 

While I don't have a solid theoretical justification for why one must allow a disconnected network, I have an anecdote: I've seen disconnected networks persistently turn out to be the most-fit genome during early evolution of a recurrent car-driving network.  Whether that is just a fluke, or a bug/idiosyncratic behavior in either neat-python or the TORCS simulator, I don't know, but if not, it seems they could be a useful stepping-stone to building a more fit, connected, network.

Even if the disconnected networks always perform more poorly than any connected network, presumably any species which produce disconnected genomes would rapidly die out and be replaced by those more likely to produce connected networks.  The current neuron/network implementations shouldn't experience any difficulties evaluating a disconnected network, but if you do have an instance in which they raise exceptions, please let me know.

If it turns out that you have problems in which a disconnected network is a big problem, one simple solution is to add a check for this condition in your fitness function, and if the network is disconnected, assign it a very low score and return.  

If there is a class of problems that require this kind of check, please reopen this item and let me know, and I can make the library configurable so that it will do the check for you without having to write any extra code.

Using `sitecustomize` is a preferred way to use `win-unicode-console`. Create module `sitecustomize.py` at `PythonXY/Lib/site-packages/` containing the following lines.

    import win_unicode_console
    win_unicode_console.enable()

The module `sitecustomize` is imported at every startup, so `win-unicode-console` is enabled every time. See https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/appendix.html#the-customization-modules.

Please also consider editing the title of this issue to more accurately describe the problem. From <https://musescore.org/en/developers-handbook/how-write-good-bug-report-step-step-instructions#Title> (**emphasis** added):

> The title should describe the problem as best as possible. **Remember that the title is read more often than any other part of the bug report.**
> 
> *Poor title*: Notes don't display correctly
> This title is not specific enough for someone to look at it a month from now, and remember what the bug report is referring to.
> 
> *Good title*: Stems too short for 32nd and 64th notes
> This title is an improvement over the previous title, because it specifies the type of notes that are affected and identifies the display problem.
> 
> After submitting the issue, it is possible to improve the title.

From <http://university.utest.com/writing-quality-bug-reports-and-utest-etiquette/> (**emphasis** added):

> The title is the face of your report. **It’s the first thing anyone sees and it’s importance cannot be overstated. A good title helps reduce duplicate issues and can quickly convey a summary of the bug.**
> 
> It’s best to avoid generic problems in the title. For example, these should never be used:
> 
> * XYX is not working properly
> * Issue with XYZ
> * XYZ is corrupted/does not look right
> 
> The above example titles add little value in describing the problem. By nature, every report is describing something that is not working as it should. Be specific about what makes it “not working.”
> 
> *Instead of*: Sorting is not working properly.
> 
> *Try*: Sorting is happening in reverse order.
> 
> *Instead of*: Issues with GUI on navigation bar.
> 
> *Try*: Navigation bar is wrapping to a second line.
> 
> Often times, bugs are migrated into the developer’s database that may contain hundreds, if not thousands, of other issues. Imagine trying to search this database for “navigation bar”. That search will return every issue related to the navigation bar. Searching for “wrapping to second line” is much more specific making it easier to find the bug. Your bug report needs to survive (and be useful) beyond the current test cycle; a strong title will help it through it’s journey.

See also <https://university.utest.com/bug-title-recommendations-from-lead-testers/>.

The easier you make it for others to help you, the more likely you'll get what you want.

@mhdonnelly first off, thanks for the PR!

I really like the idea of having an upper bound somewhere. I also think this functionality is generic enough to also have other `Target` classes benefit from this. For instance, users of `FileTarget` may also want to limit the upload size of a given file.

I would propose two options -

1. Either we move this check to the parser level. So that calls like `parser.register('name', ValueTarget())` look like `parser.register('name', ValueTarget(), max_size=42)`. This would need some internal changes - `Part` class would need to support a `max_size` attribute then, and we would need to have the `parser` support returning a list of errors it faced when working with the input. The flow might be something as follows -

```python
parser = StreamingFormDataParser(headers)
parser.register('file', FileTarget('/tmp/file.txt'), max_size=1024)
parser.data_received(chunk)

assert parser.is_done()

print(parser.get_errors()) # {'file': ['input size more than 1024 bytes']}
```

2. We move this check to the `BaseTarget`, so that other `Target` classes have this functionality built in. Additionally, I would propose that failing this check does not throw an exception, since that limits the parser being able to read other request parameters. Sure, we could have the parser catch the exception and continue, but it would make the internal parser code a bit messy.

In general (independent of this PR), my tendency is to always catch errors at the earliest possible stage they can be caught, and handle them as soon as possible. In this case, this means the first approach (move the check to the parser level).

Thoughts?

BTW, the tornado-idiomatic way to use a custom poll implementation would be:

```python
from tornado.ioloop import IOLoop
import zmq.green.eventloop.ioloop as green_ioloop
IOLoop.configure(green_ioloop.IOLoop)

loop = IOLoop.current()
```

That would not rely on zmq's implicit call to configure if you call `.instance` or `.current` on the subclasses.

I would argue that your code demonstrates an undesirable pattern of usage. Errors should be handled in a way appropriate to the condition that the error is signalling, the code that is being executed when the error occurs, and the action that the application should consequently take.

- `ServiceUnavailable` signals that the driver has been unable to communicate with the server or cluster. This could be handled by backing off and retrying later or alerting the end user to the communication failure.
- `AddressError` signals that the address of a server cannot even be resolved. This could indicate a configuration error or could request corrected input from an end user.
- `ProtocolError` happens when there is a fundamental breakdown in communication between the driver and the server. This _should_ never happen, but if it were to occur, it would indicate a bug in the driver or server software. Neither the application nor the end user can do anything about this (except perhaps work around it) and a bug report should be raised.
- `SecurityError` may indicate that the user's credentials are incorrect or have expired. Maybe a prompt to the end user for correct details is appropriate here.
- `CypherError` would occur when a Cypher statement fails. There are several reasons for this (and several appropriate subclasses) but this will often mean either a transient error (where a delay and retry is appropriate) or a fault in your application code. Unless of course the user enters Cypher directly, in which case the error should be forwarded to the user for display.

Thanks for the PR! So the way we handle changelog generation is a little different than modifying the actual changelog. We actually regenerate the changelog from changes found in the ``.changes`` directory each time we do a release. So if you only make the changes directly to ``CHANGELOG.rst`` they will be erased in the next release. The changes need to be added in the JSON as well. It looks like you will need to update ``1.4.0.json``, ``1.1.2.json``, ``1.1.0.json``, and ``0.0.21.json`` as well.

I don't think pinning the PyPI requirements is enough. It happened in the past several times that things broke when `something` was updated from v1.2.3 to v1.2.4, and if this `something` isn't a Python package, the pinning won't help to catch this.

Besides, we want to get security updates that may be coming with `Flask-Foo` v4.5.6 to v4.5.7 upgrade. So it would be good not to "overpin" things too much. We could leave some liberties to account for these cases, but we also had observed some breakage for such patchlevel updates in the past too, since not everybody uses semantic versioning strictly.

Hence, rather than pinning, I think the best would be to simply revive our good old nightly builds and test Docker, Ubuntu14, CentOS7 installation techniques every night, as we used to do with Jenkins on legacy code base. This will make sure that we have latest-greatest demo site features and that the installation procedures always work too.   

I can take care of this...

(P.S.1. Note that the demo site was meant to be an "integration testing space" for various Invenio components and features, so pinning packages wouldn't be too good from this point of view either.  Rather use latest-greatest packages and catch troubles nightly in a true CI style.)

(P.S.2. Note also that helper scripts are used in both Docker and non-Docker installation technique, so that we are fully sure that both installation methods -- native and docker -- are always OK and that one of them has not fallen apart due to being unused, as was sometimes the case in service overlays.)

> Removing these declarations is healthy, because calling `manage_changeProperties()` breaks on PythonScripts when the declarations are still in place.

The `manage_historyCopy` method is about copying forward an older revision of a TTW object.

The removed declarations are for methods inherited from `OFS.History.Historical`, where they *don't* have declarations:  the design is that the subclasses would implement / protect them.

The two "hook" methods (`manage_beforeHistoryCopy` and `manage_afterHistoryCopy`) shouldn't *ever* be public:  in fact, they should be named `_beforeHistoryCopy` and `_afterHistoryCopy` (see https://github.com/zopefoundation/Zope/issues/220).

The non-hook method (`manage_historyCopy`) *does* need to be protected, but the design of the base class was to assume that subclasses would need to assign a "custom" permission to it.  I think it is actually a bug that `AccessControl.class_init.InitializeClass` emits these warnings for inherited methods (see https://github.com/zopefoundation/AccessControl/issues/49).

**What's the disadvantage of using the index to identify which phone is which?**
Making the assumption by order is less manageable, since the assumption is satisfied in the config file and is completely obscure to a new code reader. It'll be very difficult for the reader to figure out what the test expects a device to be capable of purely by index.
Forcing tests that care about device identity to explicitly acquire device objects by 'label' or some kind of id clarifies the assumption in code, which makes the test code more maintainable.
In the meantime, we are not sacrificing any convenience for tests that do not care about the device identity. Since such tests only care about they got the right number of objects.
We do need to add an option to specify the expected number of objects in register_controller though.

To get around this I just pass in the IDs of the current fixVersions and the new one I want to add:

```
issue = jira.issue('JRA-1330')
versions = [{"id": version.id} for version in issue.fields.fixVersions]
versions.append({"id": *my_new_version_id*})
issue.update(fields=dict(fixVersions=versions))
```

From reading the S3 resource API `bucket.download_file(obj.key, filename)` there is no proper interface to pass callback or custom ThreadExecutor. Since this API is very high level, the actual logic of invoking thread pool hides really deep.

We will find some time look deeper on this issue but we don't have capacity right now to provide a fully working solution.

I suggest to start from monkey patching https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/develop/boto3/s3/transfer.py#L142 `create_transfer_manager`. In this function you can see when `use_threads = False` it uses `NonThreadedExecutor ` which only uses the main thread. This is the reason the solution from @ShibaBandit works.

By default this library `boto3 -> s3transfer` uses python built-in `futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` as you can see here: https://github.com/boto/s3transfer/blob/develop/s3transfer/futures.py#L370.

If you monkey patch the `create_transfer_manager` so that it passes your custom ThreadPoolExecutor, and the executor injects the segment to its children threads, the problem should go away. Another caveats is that when doing thread injection, thread local needs to be cleaned up when work done to avoid thread pollution, since threads in a thread pool will be re-used.

Ideally we could have provide a single line opt-in patch and everything automatically works so you don't have to care about the underlying details. But the challenge of context binding for the SDK is that every single library utilizes ThreadPool needs to be configured (or worse case like this requires monkey patching) separately.

This isn't a requests-kerberos issue, but a build issue in pykerberos. You want to report it here: https://github.com/02strich/pykerberos.

If the library can't come up with the correct service principle, the only way to force it (as of v0.11.0) without having to call the underlying kerberos libraries itself is:

given that you want the service principle to be   HTTP/poke.int.example.org@INT.EXAMPLE.ORG

```
auth=HTTPKerberosAuth(service='HTTP/poke.int.example.org',hostname_override='INT.EXAMPLE.ORG')
requests.get(url, auth=auth)
```

Having to use `patch()` after `upload_...()` unfortunately has several more or less serious drawbacks:
1. It requires the client to have credentials allowing PATCH requests, preventing immutable append-only semantics.
2. It is not atomic, so if the PATCH operation fails, or the client fails between the calls, the data in the storage service might be left in an inconsistent state.
3. It lacks consistency. There will be a window when the blob data has been uploaded but when metadata are still incorrect or missing, leading to potential race conditions.
4. The metadata-generation will never be 1.

These are currently blocking me from using gcloud-python.

Here is a proposal of how this could work. API Event will get a new property called `Cors`. Here are two ways to use it:

## Shorthand
```
Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
Properties:
  Runtime: ...
  Events:
     GetApi:
         Type: Api
         Properties:
              Cors: "*"
             Method: GET
             Path: /foo

     PostApi:
         Type: Api
         Properties:
              Cors: "http://example.com"
             Method: POST
             Path: /foo
```

This will generate CORS headers automatically, for each Path+Method combo and set Allow-Origin to "\*".  The value of Cors property is a list of origins you want to allow. Allow-Headers is always set to "\*".

## Long Form
```
Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
Properties:
  Runtime: ...
  Events:
     GetApi:
         Type: Api
         Properties:
              Cors: 
                  AllowOrigin: "List of origins"
                  AllowHeaders: "List of allowed headers"
             Method: GET
             Path: /foo

     PostApi:
         Type: Api
         Properties:
              Cors: 
                  AllowOrigin: "List of origins"
                  AllowHeaders: "List of allowed headers"
             Method: POST
             Path: /foo
```

This will generate CORS headers automatically, for each Path+Method combo and set Allow-Origin and Allow-Headers to whatever value that was specified.

It looks like the problematic line is in `tidy`:

```
        goto non-V1 [next] -> x  x delete // undouble consonant
```

The problem there is that `V1` is vowels, but `non-V1` includes digits as well as consonants, so this will undouble digits too.

Something like this would address that, and fixes your testcase:

```
--- a/algorithms/finnish/stem_ISO_8859_1.sbl
+++ b/algorithms/finnish/stem_ISO_8859_1.sbl
@@ -24,7 +24,7 @@ externals ( stem )
 integers ( p1 p2 )
 strings ( x )
 booleans ( ending_removed )
-groupings ( AEI V1 V2 particle_end )
+groupings ( AEI V1 V1D V2 particle_end )
 
 stringescapes {}
 
@@ -35,6 +35,7 @@ stringdef o"   hex 'F6'
 
 define AEI 'a{a"}ei'
 define V1 'aeiouy{a"}{o"}'
+define V1D V1 + '0123456789'
 define V2 'aeiou{a"}{o"}'
 define particle_end V1 + 'nt'
 
@@ -176,7 +177,7 @@ backwardmode (
             do ( ['j'] 'o' or 'u' delete )
             do ( ['o'] 'j' delete )
         )
-        goto non-V1 [next] -> x  x delete // undouble consonant
+        goto non-V1D [next] -> x  x delete // undouble consonant
     )
 )
 
```

An alternative would be to add a grouping with all the Finnish consonants.

I think we need to carefully review uses of `non` elsewhere (both in this and the other algorithms).  As a general feature, the the algorithms shouldn't be conflating different numbers, as that's unhelpful for their intended purpose.

> Is content not 'core'? Shouldn't our 'core' apps be namespaced under kolibri.core, and others then namespaced under kolibri.plugins?

Matter of taste I think, I like the current layout but I'm not overly attached to it.

> it makes the logic much harder to reason about and control

This was my main concern, and I would rather it is simplified.

> it invites hacks like using priority = 1.00001 or priority = 999999 as plugins compete with each other

I think just using the role as the top level sort, and then sorting alphabetically could stop this.

> I really dislike how the config is defined as subclasses of NavigationHook and UserMenuHook and then obliquely dumped into JS with json.dumps([h.menu_item for h in UserMenuHook().registered_hooks]). It makes it very difficult to understand what the schema of the config variables will be.

This could be addressed, as the `menu_item` method from the hook does not need to be there, it could be in the tag instead, so that the schema of the config variables is explicitly there, I can also add validation to it that would error out and give meaningful errors if there was a misconfigured Hook - for example, at least one of the properties is optional, so that should be more evident). As for subclassing, that's how hooks work, and they rely on the inheritance to register unique entries (I don't really want to argue about that implementation, because I didn't write it, and I am not fussed either way).


> Concretely, I'd be interested in exploring a strategy where the configuration of these menus (including ordering, divider locations, etc) is explicitly encoded in a config file with a well-defined schema, like webpack. This config file could be overwritten, extended, or transformed during the custom build process. It could even reference custom implementations of vue nav item components that have their own internal logic and references to vuex state.

This is precisely what I am trying to avoid as this gives us very little over what we are currently doing (except some slight convenience when we make a custom build for a third party). On a purely theoretical level, this still breaks the pluggability of plugins (if you deactivate the 'facility_management' plugin, its sidenav entry would still be there, whereas with this approach, it would disappear).

On a practical level, another big motivation for me of this work was to be able to include 'prototype' plugins in our distributed mainline Kolibri builds, but that are deactivated by default. These plugins can then be activated, and if they necessitate a top level side nav link, then they will be available to the end user.

* make a Floating Action Button (FAB) that contains the logo of Reconstructive or the archival system (IPWB)
* this FAB is by default placed somewhere, but it is made draggable to be placed anywhere on the page by user (the location state is then saved locally in user's browser)
* the FAB hides itself when there is no activity on the page for a while (a couple second), but reappears on any activity such as scroll, click, touch, or any keyboard activity
* when the FAB is clicked or tapped, it expands into a modal window and displays all sorts of metadata and options for interaction
* there should be an option to completely hide the banner for a period (such as a day) and can be shown again anytime using a keyboard shortcut, such state information should be saved in user's browser locally
* additionally, the banner can have four states:
  * hidden
  * FAB
  * mini strip (with only the essential info)
  * expanded (modal window)

Some inline commentary for the conclusion


- If we use numpy, it will be as an optional dependency.
  - *epage*  Our concern is for the developer who doesn't have a pre-compiled numpy for their target
- Only use numpy arrays for waveforms / samples. Not for sequence steps, filter coefficients, etc.
  - *epage* Considerations: is this config time vs inner loop?  What is the likelihood that the data will be used by or come from numpy?
-  For APIs such as NI-SCOPE that have different "fetch" functions, for different types:
  - *epage* The goal is a "ease of use" API and a "high performance" API.
  - *epage* [nidaqmx's readers/writers](https://github.com/ni/nidaqmx-python/blob/master/nidaqmx/tests/test_stream_analog_readers_writers.py#L55) are a nice approach for the high performance case because they decouple policy and attempt to expose python-like stream manipulation functions.  We recognized that they aren't a perfect fit for every API though and just having variations of the functions can be reasonable.
  - Expose only the scaled double version, and have it return list
    - *epage* The ease of use function we mentioned.  Variants that are meant for performance are left off because that is a numpy thing.
  - Create fetch_into that take in numpy arrays. These will call the right underlying function based on datatype
    - *epage* DAQmx has just a `read` function (on its numpy reader) that can both allocate or reuse a buffer.  We (or at least I) felt it simplifies the API to have an `into` function which I've seen precedence for elsewhere.  If someone cares about performance, they probably also care about their buffer management.

Alright, here is an update:

* The `WebSocketResource` should have been fixed for current Twisted with https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/pull/705 - This provides support for Twisted Web being in charge (sitting on the listening endpoint), and Autobahn being switched over to for WebSocket - via `WebSocketResource`
* Crossbar.io now has `UniSocketServerProtocol` https://github.com/crossbario/crossbar/blob/master/crossbar/router/unisocket.py - This provides support for having a single listening WAMP endpoint for routers in Crossbar.io that can talk WAMP over RawSocket and WebSocket, while at the same time serving Twisted Web resource trees. This is using the other approach: Autobahn listening, and then switching over to Twisted Web.

I think supporting both directions for integration between Twisted and Autobahn like in above is a good approach and should cover most users needs in this area.

Of course, both features internally use "hacks" (unsupported, non-official "API" in Twisted) - and we should use official Twisted machinery once it is there. For now, "it works" ..

Ok. I figured this out:

A scheme is not required to use `//` after it. Therefore, go parses anything prior to the first `:` as the scheme.

So `kiddie-armchair.192.168.64.2.xip.io` is parsed as the scheme, and `31258` as the host. This is probably a wontfix by golang as they are complaint with the spec.

@mboersma For this particular location, you could just prepend `http://`, since the api never returns a scheme. However, if we want to support the controller running on a non-standard port as well, we will need to factor `url.Parse` out of the entire codebase.

@jannikluhn 
 > Some fields have type uint256, which I assume means that encoded integers are padded with zeros to a size of 32 bytes. This makes sense for the SMC (easier to decode) but not so much for RLP encoding (takes unnecessary space). Can we encode integers for the RLP format in the traditional way (like block headers are today) and then provide some kind of "encode for SMC" method?

If we do this, it seems
1. we will need to implement the minimal rlp decoder for `big_endian_int`. I'm not sure if it is worthwhile or not.
2. Or we just use the rlp decoder contract. But it needs an additional contract call.

> Related: What's the hash of a collation header? A block header hash is keccak(rlp(header)), in the existing collation implementation it's keccak(shard_id ++ period ++ ...). Do we want to have both, should the SMC learn RLP, or should we go with the field-concatenated hash everywhere?

IMO for simplicity, using field-concatenated hash seems easier. I'm not sure if it is worthwhile to make SMC able to RLP encode. We will have to implement it and test it further. And maybe it will cost more gas to do rlp encoding compared to the field-concatenated hash.

> There's no reference to the main chain anymore (in the previous version there used to be a "period_start_prevhash"). Is that intentional?

I'm not sure of it either. Maybe need to ask @hwwhww 

> Is there any format for the proposer signature? I think at least a maximum size would be necessary, but we could also go further and have v/r/s integers just like (old-style) transactions (although I kind of like having a single signature field).

I think we can use the previous format of signature. It is the just the concatenation of `v`, `r`, `s`. (https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/blob/develop/old_sharding_poc/sharding/contract_utils.py#L19-L22). And it can be verified through the [validation code](https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/blob/develop/old_sharding_poc/sharding/validator_manager_utils.py#L44-L59) we used before.

> What's the proposer signature exactly? Same as transaction signature, but without the replay protection hacks?

I don't know it for now either. It is not currently included in the spec and need to be clarified as well. If we want to do it now, maybe we can start off letting the `sig_hash_of_proposer` to be `keccak(concat(shard_id ++ parent_hash ++ ... ++ proposer_bid))` or something like this.

Just some thoughts. Please correct me if there is anything wrong :)
And we can discuss about these on the meeting tomorrow.

I think there are a few considerations here:

* dependency specifications should have the option of saying from which channel they expect a specific package to come from, rather than "from whatever set of channels you happen to have setup"

* dependency specifications should provide a mechanism to state exactly *or* optionally which channels should be part of the package search (and those channels themselves can be specified either explicitly with a full URL or partially with just a channel name).

Here is an example showing off both these ideas in a format that is **not** currently valid conda JSON:

```json
channels:
    - anaconda
    - r
    - http://anaconda.org/ijstokes

requirements:
  build:
    - python
    - setuptools
    - anaconda/numpy
  run:
    - python
    - anaconda/numpy
```

With an option for "exclusivity" of channels by using `exclusive_channels:` instead of `channels`, meaning **only** that listed set of channels will be considered (ignore `.condarc` and any `defaults`).

I believe I finally found the root cause of the remaining test failure:

The reason why conda expects to find the explicitly specified package in its own package cache is because it actually copies and extracts it there beforehand. When it does this, it adds a record in `urls.txt` for it. However, conda tries to detect if a package with the same name and md5 checksum exists already in some package cache. If yes, it takes the `urls.txt` entry from that package cache, otherwise it records the path of the explicitly specified package. I assume it thinks that the former is more reliable or more permanent.

This PR changed this behavior because before conda could not find a matching package in the package cache because it moved the old tarball out of the way. With this PR however, it takes the md5 checksum from the extracted directory (if one exists). Note that this only occurs in a scenario where a package was installed from a regular source and then is overwritten with an explicit install of the same package from a different location. Since conda now finds a matching package cache entry, it takes its source (which is regular) and records it in the `urls.txt` instead of the location of the explicitly specified package.

And this is exactly what causes the failure because now the `Dist()` created from the package cache has a different channel than the `Dist()` that conda constructs in `conda/misc.py`.

There are probably various ways to fix or work around this. For this PR I chose one that requires minimal changes to existing logic: when looking for package cache entries matching the explicit package, just ignore the package cache that the explicit package is being written into. This basically restores the previous behavior where that package cache was ignored simply because the tarball was moved out of the way before running the match.

The overall logic here seems kind of fragile though. I'm confident that it is possible to construct a test that would lead to the same error both with and without this PR: Construct two package caches A and B both of which contain the same package "X" and then attempt to install "X" through an explicit install. It would write into one of the package caches but when doing the matching would find a match in the other.

To fix this at the source I see two possible solutions:

 * Don't try to be smart and simply always record the origin of the explicitly specified package in the `urls.txt` file.

 * Don't rely on the entry in the package cache to match exactly the constructed `Dist` object when doing explicit installs. Instead, simply link the `Dist` object from the package cache with the same `dist_name` as the explicit package. This then relies on the fact that we just installed the explicitly specified package in the first writable package cache.

From these two I think I prefer the first option because it seems more robust. But I might be missing some details of why this matching is being done in the first place, and maybe there are other options available as well. Anyway, this is probably material for another PR.

I keep NEON vectorisation in minDot/maxDot only - there it make sense - it's batch operation and most of time is faster than scalar version. Here is example of `AccessLogger` which produces messages exactly same like current `aiohttp.helpers.AccessLogger` with default `log_format`

```python
class AccessLogger(AbstractAccessLogger):

    def log(self, request, response, time):
        msg = '{request.remote} - - {t} "{r}" {response.status} {response.body_length} "{referrer}" "{user_agent}"'

        r = '%s %s HTTP/%s.%s' % tuple((request.method,
                                        request.path_qs) + request.version)

        t = datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime('[%d/%b/%Y:%H:%M:%S +0000]')

        self.logger.info(msg.format(
            t=t,
            r=r,
            referrer=request.headers.get('Referrer', '-'),
            user_agent=request.headers.get('User-Agent', '-'),
            request=request,
            response=response,
            time=time,
        ))
```

I've played a bit with this `AccessLogger`, and server can be speeded up to 5% in comparison to the default `aiohttp.helpers.AccessLogger`, but it is not big deal.

Most useful example is something like:

```python

class AccessLogger(AbstractAccessLogger):

    def log(self, request, response, time):
        self.logger.info(f'{request.remote} "{request.method}" -> {response.status}')
```

Absolutely :) gut feeling would be not a huge amount, since most of the functionality described is already present, with the already-noted exception that we used yaml instead of json. But, I don't see it being a huge amount of effort to add support for json (which I also prefer over yaml in this case, since most end users view the yaml contents through the UI of the configurator anyway), and we could either keep yaml support in parallel, or replace it as desired. The main thing to do first would be to agree on the config file discovery mechanism(s) (currently we attempt to load *all* yaml/yml in nbextensions_path, but it would probably be good to restrict file naming if we go for json) and structure (keys, values, and their uses). I think my misunderstanding comes from the idea that if you have a computation graph `a->b->c`, `grad` transforms it into `a->b->c->c'->b'->a'`, while Rop transforms it into `a"->b"->c"` (where " is JVP), so wouldn't `grad(Rop(f, v))` "double" the size of the graph while `grad(dot(v, grad(f)))` "doubles" and then "doubles" it again? But @j-towns's ASCII art makes it clear that both forward and reverse mode double the number of nodes in the graph, so they're of similar computational cost in terms of FLOPs.

You can also use `grad(fun, 1)` or `grad(fun, argnum=1)` to get the gradient with respect to the second positional argument (i.e. argument number 1), or any other argument number. Check out the functions in convenience_wrappers.py too, since we have some other convenient ways to compute gradients, like `multigrad` and `multigrad_dict`.

This means that I may need the `/data` bind point to be available on each container. However, it does appears that in singularity 2.3 that if the bind point does not exist it will by default use `src` as the mount point within the container.

Example:
`singularity exec --bind $PWD:/data docker://realtimegenomics/rtg-tools rtg vcfeval`
since the `/data` mount point doesn't exist within the rtg-tools container, singularity will instead set the mount point to `$PWD` within the container.

Adding a page here for instructions about how to compile and ship. Feel free to augment it with your experiences, and I'll fill in missing bits best I can.

https://github.com/pyqt/python-qt5/wiki/Updating-the-repository

For reference, this is what I had to do to get this working in a Virtualenv:

1. `pip install git+git://github.com/pyqt/python-qt5.git`
2. `mv /path/to/virtualenv/qt.conf /path/to/virtualenv/Scripts`
3. Edit `/path/to/virtualenv/Scripts/qt.conf` to contain following:
```
[Paths]
Prefix = ../Lib/site-packages/PyQt5
Binaries = ../Lib/site-packages/PyQt5
```
4. `set "PYTHONPATH=/path/to/virtualenv/Lib/site-packages/PyQt5"`
5. Download PyQt5 5.71 wheel for Python 3 from https://pypi.python.org/pypi/PyQt5/5.7.1 and extract.
6. Copy `resources` and `translations` from the extracted wheel (`\PyQt5\Qt`) to `/path/to/virtualenv/Lib/site-packages/PyQt5`

Et voila. I was able to load a web page in `QWebEnginePage`

i personally don't see as a wrong replacement. the xunit report is just generated at the very end of the process and it's a consolidated report with all errors (split by test, of course).
i am not proposing a replacement at all. i am just proposing to output those errors on-the-fly (and not just at the end), in the same way you guys do with the screenshots and the html :)
this makes it easier and faster to check the failure stacktrace (we can't check then while the tests are still running) and also somehow covers the scenario where jenkins/process might fail before the xunit report is generated.
as i said, it's mainly useful when we have a long test suite run (e.g. our whole test suite here takes 1h+ to complete). in this scenario, having access to some failure stacktrace becomes very handy.

The two primary priorities are 1) the maintenance of ICANN/IANA TLDs and 2) the general maintenance of the list.

Those two points have higher priorities over any private TLD. These tasks include:

- keeping the list in sync with all the new GTLDs approved almost weekly by IANA (publicsuffix#185)
- keeping the list accurate with the various registry changes (e.g. publicsuffix#187)
- deal with existing entries (e.g. publicsuffix#206)

All these tasks already take a huge amount of time, especially when it comes to research and investigate authoritative sources. In most cases, we have to contact directly the registries and go back-and-forth with them.

It's a time consuming effort. And to be clear, maintaining the list is none of the maintainers full-time job.

subscribe() starts the high-level balanced consumer, joins the configured group.id, and waits for a partition assignment based on its subscribed topics. When an assignment is received it either calls assign() automatically or lets you do it in the on_assign callback.

assign() is the lower level consumer that actually starts consuming the given set of partitions (after stopping any previous consumption). The group.id does not have any relevance for this call since it all it operates on is a definitive set of partitions that comes from somewhere (high-level consumer assignment, user manual assignment, etc..). You are free to call assign() at any time, more or less, to replace the current set of partitions being consumed.

Having said that, a group.id must still be configured for assign() to function, but this is due to internal implementation details. Unless you join the group (subscribe()) or commit offsets the group.id is not actually used.

There is some pep8 rule about allowing mutable defaults in function signatures, I'll try to find it. But whenever I try to set an empty dict as an argument default I get that warning from pylint, pyflakes, pep8, etc.

Instead you could add a line that if the arg is `None` then set it to an empty dict inside the function def.

Note when modules are imported the function and class definitions are read, but not their contents. Maybe that's why; having a lot of mutables that can basically never go out of scope would take up resources and make reference counting tricky. Not to mention that having reference that pass through functions can cause issues too, IE: basically another method could change the contents of that reference (from empty `dict` to something else) then that would be tricky to troubleshoot/debug.

But the contents inside a function def are only read when the function is called, and for the most part any reference inside the function is trashed when the function returns and it's references go out of scope. Only globals and argument references survive, which is why they're tricky.

I am hesitant to promote writing `sacred` experiments in notebooks because I cannot see a good way to keep the results reproducible.
The code in a notebook can be executed and changed in arbitary order. So it is unclear how to rerun an experiment even if sacred would save the whole notebook in the database.
I prefer the workflow of keeping the experiment in a separate file and then import and call it from a notebook. That way I can interactively explore the results while keeping the results reproducible. 

That being said, if you say that you want to use sacred in a notebook nonetheless,  I'd be happy to remove the dependency on an existing filename. That shouldn't be hard to do.

Unfortunately, there is no convenient way of doing this right now. You could use a named config which behaves like config updates from the command-line and is thus executed before the ingredient config scopes. So in your main file you could put:
``` python
@ing.named_config
def foo():
    a = 8
```
But you would still have to add that named config from the run command or the command-line, using `experiment.py with foo`. This might shorten your typing effort but still doesn't fully automate it. So your solution might still be the best for that usecase.

Side note: This lack of configuration flexibility is one of the reasons why I am unhappy with ingredients, as they are. Ideally, you should be able to configure them even using entries from your main config. But I'm still in the process of figuring out a good and convenient way to do that.

When you're using CALLLESS, you're making a fundamental change to how angr interprets the program bytecode. The only thing it does is to trigger a tiny bit of logic to pop the return address and unconstrain the return value every time it sees a call instruction. This _very_ heavily relies on the linkage between the call instruction and an actual function call. Examples of where this linkage breaks, and this option will not actually just skip function calls, include using `call $+5` as `push eip` and tail calls.

The CFG uses its own approach to deal with these. I'm not the CFG expert, but my understanding is that it does the following:

- does NOT use `CALLLESS`, but instead uses `DO_RET_EMULATION`, which adds the fake return in addition to each call
- requires that an analysis of the called function complete and determine that it is possible to return from the called function
- detect the `push eip` idiom manually
@rhelmot thanks, I''ll check ASAP. Should I install the latest angr or a specific PR?

First I have to admit that the code I wrote was pretty bad. `edges` is a list of 3-tuples: `(source node, destination node, a dict of all labels associated with the edge)`. A `fake_ret` edge is an edge between call site and (the supposedly) return target. If there is a 'fake_ret` edge associated to the source basic block node, *and* the calling target is successfully resolved when doing CFG recovery, there should be at least one `call` edge associated with the source basic block node and each calling target (or multiple `call` edges if there are more than one calling target are resolved, which I don't think `CFGFast` is doing by default).

My guess is that the target address is not the beginning of a function. It's either because `CFGFast` failed to resolve the target before, or the resolution was right, but the target address simply does not exist. I don't know if my guess holds unless you find the root cause, or post a minimal test case that reproduces this issue.

> I can fix this issue by searching for the member of edges which is of class angr.knowledge.function.Function.

I believe every single node in `Function.transition_graph` and `Function.graph` must be a `CodeNode` (either a `BlockNode` or a `HookNode`, which are both subclasses of `CodeNode`). You won't be able to find `Function` instances in `transition_graph` or `graph`.

> I found that every time we introduce a variable (initial=true), we have a node with the same location, but with a SimTempVariable as its successor, and initial=false. 

This is expected. What you see here comes from the IR statement `t153 = LDle:I32(t151)`, which creates two different `SimAction`s:
- loading from memory location `t151`, and
- writing the value to `t153`.

`initial` implies "a new variable, probably an input value with respect to this block, the current function, or whatever we are analyzing, is introduced". I guess during the analysis, we've never read from the location that `t151` points to, that's why it introduces a new `SimMemoryVariable` with `initial` being True. Then the value is written to a `SimTemporaryVariable`, at which moment we are not introducing a new value, but assigning an existing value to `t153`.

Since it's unclear how and where the joiner credential should be used with openthread APIs and test environment, I've changed the APIs to use binary PSKd + length, removed all PSKd conversion (decoding) and added TODOs (with commented-out blocks of code) on the end-nodes of the API, where the PSK strings are passed in.

To summarize the changes:
* aPSKd string replaced with aPSKd byte array and length in the API calls
* Added/modified windows IOCTL structures to accommodate PSKd length.
* Added PSKd length checks in every API call (may be redundant in many cases).
* Corrected definitions/constants related to PSKd length
* Added base32-thread encode and decode implementation (only used in tests)
* Added foundation for base32 encode/decode unit tests (a standard with official test-cases is needed).

Also, the spinel protocol passes the PSKd as a string in **PROP_THREAD_JOINERS**. I would consider it a "core" layer, rather than UI, so I think it should send the binary PSKd with length, but was reluctant to do any changes as there's an ongoing standardization taking place. IMO, joiner credential decoding should only take place at the user interface level, so please consider changing **PROP_THREAD_JOINERS** data structure from **"A(t(ULE))"** to **"A(t(dLE))"**.

This is used on Linux-based platforms where the interface to the NCP is SPI. `wpantund` can be configured to talk to a separate process instead of a UART. `spi-hdlc-adapter` allows `wpantund` to communicate with the NCP as if it were connected via UART, even though it is physically connected via SPI.

At some point native support for the [Spinel SPI protocol recommendation](https://cdn.rawgit.com/openthread/openthread/a3b464044eaecd911148b24eba9d09e532e79e1e/doc/draft-spinel-protocol.html#rfc.appendix.A.2)(which is what `spi-hdlc-adapter` uses) might be added to `wpantund`, but this solution works well enough for now and is rather convenient since it doesn't require any significant changes to `wpantund`. You can even tunnel such a connection across SSH.

You got this error if you are running more than one time this code, because every time, data are added to the default graph, so it has a lot of input data layers, but you only feed a single X, thus the error.

To correct it, if you want to run this code multiple times, you need to specify a graph:
```
import tflearn
import tensorflow as tf
​
X = [3.3,4.4,5.5,6.71,6.93,4.168,9.779,6.182,7.59,2.167,7.042,10.791,5.313,7.997,5.654,9.27,3.1]

​
graph_to_use = tf.Graph()
with graph_to_use.as_default():
  input_layer = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None])
  linear_layer = tflearn.single_unit(input_layer)
  regression_layer = tflearn.regression(linear_layer, optimizer="sgd", loss="mean_square",
                                                              metric="R2", learning_rate=.01)
  m = tflearn.DNN(regression_layer)
  m.fit(X, Y, n_epoch=1000, show_metric=False, snapshot_epoch=False)
```

### Option 3: We proxy normal web traffic

We take the vhost that normally serves HTTP content for the domain (if it exists), remove any matching `ServerName` and `ServerAlias` directives, and add a new `ServerName`/`ServerAlias` for an invalid domain name like `certbot.invalid`.

Finally, we set up a new vhost for the challenge domain(s) with content like:
```
<VirtualHost *:80>
    ServerName example.com
    Alias /.well-known/acme-challenge /tmp/foo
    ProxyPass "/.well-known/acme-challenge" !
    ProxyPass "/" "http://localhost/"
    RequestHeader set Host certbot.invalid
    ProxyPreserveHost On
</VirtualHost>
```
The way this works is if the request is for the challenge file, we serve it, otherwise, we proxy the traffic back to Apache but change the host header to a dummy value which causes it to be handled by the original vhost which we modified. If there are multiple domains requested and they are in different virtualhost blocks, we'll need a different dummy domain name for each block and and separate challenge vhost.

Pros of this approach:
* I am unaware of a way this can stop us from serving the challenge.

Cons of this approach:
* While probably fairly unlikely, firewall rules may prevent us from proxying the traffic back to ourselves.
* If the site normally relies on the `Host` header for more than just vhost matching, setting it back to its correct value so it doesn't break anything may be hard.
* We may need/want to do extra work to make sure there are no extra headers modified by `mod_proxy` if possible.
* We need `mod_headers`, `mod_proxy`, and `mod_proxy_http` to be installed and enabled/enableable. This is the case on all the distros I've looked at, but still.
* We should make sure we properly implement `--http-01-address` (in theory we should do this anyway) in case Apache isn't listening on localhost.

### Option 4: Redirect to service hosting challenge files

We have our Apache plugin upload the contents of the file it should serve for the challenge to an external server and include something like this:
```
<Location /.well-known/acme-challenge>
    Redirect https://http01.certbot.eff.org/toiu41ht9814gjn2o
</Location>
```

The pros of this approach are:
* I have yet to see an Apache directive that breaks this approach.

Unfortunately, that's the end of the pros. The cons are:

* If this external service goes down, people cannot obtain certificates.
* If we're starting our own service, we have to maintain it.
* If we're not starting our own service, we need to be worried about things like API changes and reliability.
* This external service will be able to tell that someone is running Certbot Apache at the IP that uploads the challenge contents.
* People behind something like the great firewall may be unable to reach the service we use.

Discussed with @atumanov yesterday, and it turns out to be more interesting than I had thought. The specific use case we discussed is to deploy new code and rolling update the driver:

- Driver runs tasks remotely on ray cluster
- Driver is deployed as microservice
- When new version of code is available, the code has to be deployed to ray cluster, as well as creating new containers
- Ray cluster and micro service need to be updated atomicly, otherwise the code run in/between the nodes and microservices will be inconsistent
- NFS file sync might help to minimize gap of deploying time (aka down time of the service), but might not help for rolling update
- The major difference for deploying the services on ray v.s traditional distributed service is that, the communication between nodes for former uses remote function call, while the later uses rpc call

Proposal:
Report doesn't actually have to keep track of every message or association since we only ever use the report as a summary. For example, we print out only 10 taxons, subjects and objects in the summary; so we only need the first 10 and we're done. We do print out each message, but honestly it's the same sort of format as a logger. Why don't we just use a logger for messages instead of the report? The logger can be configured in the same way (file vs stdout vs stderr) that the current Report is too. 

We should also be clear whether or not we need the associations. If we don't need them, let's not build them. If we do need them, let's write them out in a buffered manner. 

All of these should be configured by subcommands/options in the CLI.

from ipywidgets import IntSlider, Output, VBox
signalslider = IntSlider(description="Signal",max=len(signals)-1)
channelslider = IntSlider(description="Channel",max=0)

def update_channels(change):
    signal = signals[change.new]
    channelslider.max = signal.shape[0]-1
    # reset channel value?

def update_plot(change):
    output.clear_output(wait=True)
    with output:
        signal = signals[signalslider.value]
        plt.plot(signal[channelslider.value])
        plt.show()
signalslider.observe(update_plot, 'value')
channelslider.observe(update_plot, 'value')

update_plot(None)
VBox([signalslider, channelslider, output])
```

So usually for getting a gene x cell matrix you still need to supply a file with valid Barcodes but since people running Drop-seq will have this list anyways it would be no extra effort for them to get it. If the user just supplies a list of Cell barcodes with one Cell barcode per row to velocyto, that would be the easiest for most Drop-seq users I believe.

Regarding the Cell-ranger questions, I have actually never analyzed 10x data with CellRanger so I don't really know what steps are in that downstream pipeline. The only extra tags in the Drop-seq BAMs should be XM and XC as far as I know. 

The UMIs are error corrected in the sense that they are merged based on Hamming distance and that some general synthesis errors that might come from the beads. Besides that, I don't think there is any specific correction of UMIs.

Not super clear from how you formatted the text of the issue, but I guess it  is vlm.default_filter_and_norm() that is throwing the error not vlm.normalize("S", size=True, log=True).

If that is the case keep in mind that methods in velocyto are supposed to be called in a specific order for everything to work correctly. Are you following the order shown I  the notebooks or the tutorial? Also the methods starting with "defaults"  are kind of macroa. They logically pack a set of methods calls using some heuristic as arguments and are more intended as a reference then a facility. The idea is that users can look at the source code and try to adapt the steps to their pipeline. I should be more clear about it in the tutorial. 

In the particular case there seems to be some NaN generation due to a division by zero. Of course this should not happen. Probably the heuristic for the feature selection is too permissive for your dataset.
Fixed by adding `--custom-mirror` flag to `pyensembl install` and uploading files to github releases.
